notes.body: assigned to @jeldergl

## 📦 Minor release ([conventional commits](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/blob/main/doc/contributing/commits.md)) This Merge Request will trigger a _minor release_ of GitLab UI, triggered by commit: de760bf0ac69aecfcf6b4c2e7a2ec45ce357e0e4 This will bump the second part of the version number, e.g. `v1.2.3` -> `v1.3.0`. ## Reviewer roulette Changes that require review have been detected! A merge request is normally reviewed by both a reviewer and a maintainer in its primary category and by a maintainer in all other categories. To spread load more evenly across eligible reviewers, Danger has picked a candidate for each review slot. Feel free to [override these selections](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/projects/#gitlab-ui) if you think someone else would be better-suited or use the [GitLab Review Workload Dashboard](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/?currentProject=gitlab-ui) to find other available reviewers. To read more on how to use the reviewer roulette, please take a look at the [Engineering workflow](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/workflow/#basics) and [code review guidelines](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/development/code_review.html). Please consider assigning a reviewer or maintainer who is a [domain expert](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/projects/#gitlab-ui) in the area of the merge request. Once you've decided who will review this merge request, mention them as you normally would! Danger does not automatically notify them for you. | Category | Reviewer | Maintainer | | -------- | -------- | ---------- | | ~"frontend" | `@dmishunov` [![profile link](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/status-png/_user.png){width=12}](https://gitlab.com/dmishunov) ![current availability](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/status-png/dmishunov.png){width=12} (UTC+1, 7 hours ahead of author) | `@jivanvl` [![profile link](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/status-png/_user.png){width=12}](https://gitlab.com/jivanvl) ![current availability](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/status-png/jivanvl.png){width=12} (UTC-6, same timezone as author) | ## UX Review | Component | Reviewer | | --------- | -------- | | ~"component:drawer" | [`@andyvolpe`](https://gitlab.com/andyvolpe) | If your Merge Request changes one or more components, please have it reviewed by a Product Designer. One should have been suggested above. Otherwise, or if they are not available, feel free to assign to a UX Foundations designer: * [`@chrismicek`](https://gitlab.com/chrismicek) * [`@aregnery`](https://gitlab.com/aregnery) * [`@jeldergl`](https://gitlab.com/jeldergl) * [`@danmh`](https://gitlab.com/danmh) * [`@igloude`](https://gitlab.com/igloude) **If needed, you can retry the [🔁 `danger-review` job](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/jobs/8442732194) that generated this comment.**

Generated by :no_entry_sign: Danger
requested review from @danmh, @seggenberger, and @sdejonge

Thanks @jeldergl! Added some comments let me know what you think.

> **Note:** There should be no apparent visual changes to light mode, even if design tokens have been updated.

These changes remove the sidebar variant background color https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-ui/?path=/story/base-drawer--sidebar-variant is this intentional?

I think an integration MR may make sense here (or even just a patch of the styles in GitLab tbh) to validate how content renders in dark mode on the lighter background color. My thinking here is avoiding issues we faces with `GlModal`, although this is a `color.neutral.900` surface so it may be fine.
@jeldergl I left some code suggestions to use `@apply` otherwise LGTM
mentioned in issue gitlab-org/gitlab-services/design.gitlab.com#1886
changed the description

@danmh I realize that these aren't paired with `section` background tokens, but I don't think they need to be as they serve different purposes. The descriptions also don't indicate that pairing is essential. Is this your understanding of these as well? The background is overlapping content, while this border is defining sections within the drawer itself. Since the drawer can often be visually busy, this border recedes from the content.
I also realize that proceeding here would require an update in the UI kit.

@danmh here's how I'm thinking about this…

The background is an attribute of the drawer, the border is an attribute of separating things _within_ the drawer. If this border were for the edge/perimeter of the drawer then I would feel different about the concept of pairing here.

>I can't help but wonder if we are making these rationalisations based on the desired appearance, rather than the desired semantics

Is there a conceptual difference between using `background.color.overlap` with `border.color.default` (and not `background.color.default`) and what is here? In either case there is no pair — background and border are doing different things and applied to different elements (overlap and separation of items _within_ overlap) rather than singularly defining a box and its border. I think desired appearance can't be dismissed. Here though, I think there are different elements (box and its contents) where pairing doesn't apply to child elements.

> The background is an attribute of the drawer, the border is an attribute of separating things _within_ the drawer. If this border were for the edge/perimeter of the drawer then I would feel different about the concept of pairing here.

@jeldergl yes I think this is a great distinction and helps with some of the framing. Thank you for articulating it.

I think there is a conceptual difference. But this conceptual difference might not matter in this particular case.

Here's how I'd think through the application:

- A drawer is on top of the page content, so `*.overlap` is the right semantics.
- Identify that a border to separate the title area and the body can help hierarchy and support behaviours like scrolling.
- Ask, does an overlap meaning have specific border rules? That is look for `border.color.overlap`
- No. Ask should the boundary between the title have more or less emphasis than the default to help the interactions be understood?
- No. Use `border.color.default`.

You sum it up really well—

> In either case there is no pair — background and border are doing different things and applied to different elements (overlap and separation of items _within_ overlap) rather than singularly defining a box and its border.

But for `section` I might think differently:

- A drawer is on top of the page content, so `*.overlap` is the right semantics.
- The title area is a separate section to distinguish it from drawer body to help hierarchy and support behaviours like scrolling.
- What are the styling rules for `section`? Use all relevant `*.section` design tokens.

But these are just my opinions, and don't match how we've described the concept of section, so shouldn't block this.

---

> I think desired appearance can't be dismissed.

Strong agree. Our design medium is software and the visual outcome forms a massive part of the overall experience for many users. Perhaps where we differ slightly is the following separation of concerns for semantic design tokens:

- drawer (or any other component) should be concerned with identifying the right semantics for the different parts, that is applying the right design tokens
- design tokens should be concerned with identifying the right visual output for the different semantics

Based on this, if something doesn't look right it is for the design token to change its values, or to not use a design token at all. I think it should not choose a different semantic for an element to achieve the desired visual outcome in one or more modes.
@danmh I put some time on the calendar to walk through this. I think we'll encounter something similar with the dropdowns that will similarly use the overlap background, but have the darker borders.

changed this line in [version 3 of the diff](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4775/diffs?diff_id=1188166914&start_sha=ed35618b892ffb163b2bc44918a586a46769b6b0#3072a9eb8854e3a229dc0b07e4be6532b2eda692_62_62)
@jeldergl is it worth capturing here (for future us) why you went with default in the end?

Sure thing @danmh.

At a very high level, the decision to use default here is because section has a specific semantic meaning that didn't pair well with overlap. In working on https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-services/design.gitlab.com/-/issues/1962 and https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4818 we concluded that default should be able to work anywhere. I think that's a better fit here, and if a different aesthetic for the borders is desired that might work similarly to section borders (knockout effect), then it would be better to create a contextual design token than rely on something else with a specific semantic meaning.

Upon further design review, I also felt that the use of the default border better complimented the navigation sidebar at the other side of the page that uses lighter borders as dividers.

2. Explictly setting styles for `.gl-drawer-header-sticky` for both default and sidebar variants

mentioned in merge request gitlab!173313

@sdejonge and @seggenberger changes have been applied here. I've started an integration branch at https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/merge_requests/173313 to do more testing in the product before proceeding with a merge.

I tested a few drawer instances in the integration branch and they look as expected. I was surprised that the help one also has an overlay, but that's separate from this.

After reviewing several drawer instances I believe this is good to proceed with a review. FYI @danmh

Thanks for the integration MR exploration @jeldergl! Added a couple code suggestions.

Confirming the change in "sidebar" variant background to be consistent? Seems sensible to me, just want to make sure it's intentional.
@sdejonge yes, the sidebar variant should visually be the same other than width.
@jeldergl LGTM ✅
